result.markdown:
---
title: "Point Cloud Analysis"
---

The [Reality Capture](reality-capture.qmd) API can output a point cloud alongside (or instead of) a mesh. Once you've converted it to LAS format, `lidR` [@R-lidR] takes over, height statistics, density maps, canopy models, voxel volumes. Visualisations use `ggplot2` [@R-ggplot2]. This is the go-to tool for site survey analysis.

## Getting the Point Cloud

Request `rcs` format when creating your photoscene:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(AutoDeskR)

resp    <- getToken(id     = Sys.getenv("client_id"),
                    secret = Sys.getenv("client_secret"),
                    scope  = "data:read data:write")
myToken <- resp$content$access_token

ps <- createPhotoscene(name = "site-2026-q2", token = myToken)
# ... uploadImages(), processPhotoscene(), waitForPhotoscene() ...

result  <- checkPhotoscene(photoscene_id = myPhotosceneId, token = myToken)
rcs_url <- result$content$photoscene$scenelink

download.file(url      = rcs_url,
              destfile = "site-survey.rcs",
              headers  = c(Authorization = paste("Bearer", myToken)))
```
:::


::: callout-warning
**`lidR` reads LAS/LAZ, not RCS.** Convert the downloaded `.rcs` file to LAS using [CloudCompare](https://www.cloudcompare.org/) (free and open source) before proceeding. In CloudCompare: *File → Open* the RCS file, then *File → Save As* → LAS format.
:::

## Reading the Point Cloud


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(lidR)
pc <- readLAS("site-survey.las")
pc
#> class        : LAS (v1.3 format 1)
#> memory       : 247.8 Mb
#> extent       : 406325.4, 406412.8, 5765842.6, 5765901.3
#> coord. ref.  : WGS 84 / UTM zone 32N
#> area         : 4064.9 units²
#> points       : 3.62 million pts (1 return)
```
:::


## Height Statistics

Normalise to ground level before computing anything height-related:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pc_norm <- normalize_height(pc, knnidw())

mean(pc_norm$Z)
#> [1] 4.12    # mean height above ground (m)
sd(pc_norm$Z)
#> [1] 2.87

quantile(pc_norm$Z, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.95))
#>   25%   50%   75%   95%
#>  1.20  3.44  6.11 11.83
```
:::


## Height Distribution

@fig-height-dist shows the distribution of normalised point heights. The bulk of returns between 0–2 m are ground-level; the secondary peak around 8–12 m corresponds to roof surfaces.


::: {.cell}

:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data.frame(z = z_sample), aes(x = z)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.25, fill = "#367ABF", colour = "white") +
  labs(title = "Point Cloud Height Distribution",
       subtitle = "Normalised above ground level",
       x = "Height (m)", y = "Point count") +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Height distribution of normalised point cloud returns (above ground level).](point-cloud-analysis_files/figure-epub/fig-height-dist-1.png){#fig-height-dist}
:::
:::


::: callout-note
**Representative result.** This histogram is built from synthetic returns so it
renders without a LAS file. The same `ggplot2` code produces the equivalent chart
from your own normalised point cloud.
:::

## Canopy Height Model

Rasterise the maximum Z per cell to produce a canopy height model, great for visualising roof heights or vegetation structure across the site:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
chm <- rasterize_canopy(pc_norm, res = 0.5, algorithm = p2r())
plot(chm, col = height.colors(50), main = "Canopy Height Model (0.5 m resolution)")
```
:::


## Point Density Grid


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
density <- grid_density(pc, res = 1)
plot(density, col = gray.colors(50), main = "Point Density (pts/m²)")

mean(density[], na.rm = TRUE)
#> [1] 891.3   # points per square metre
```
:::


## Voxel Volume


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
vox        <- voxelize_points(pc_norm, res = 0.25)
volume_m3  <- nrow(vox) * 0.25^3
cat("Estimated volume:", round(volume_m3, 1), "m³\n")
#> Estimated volume: 1483.2 m³
```
:::


## Summary Table

Pull the headline metrics together into a single table for the survey report:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
metrics <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Total points", "Survey area (m²)", "Mean density (pts/m²)",
             "Mean height (m)", "95th-pct height (m)", "Voxel volume (m³)"),
  Value  = c(npoints(pc), area(pc), mean(density[], na.rm = TRUE),
             mean(pc_norm$Z), quantile(pc_norm$Z, 0.95), volume_m3)
)

knitr::kable(metrics, align = c("l", "r"))
```
:::


@tbl-pc-summary shows the result for the example site survey used throughout this chapter.


::: {#tbl-pc-summary .cell tbl-cap='Point cloud summary metrics for the example site survey.'}
::: {.cell-output-display}


|Metric                |     Value|
|:---------------------|---------:|
|Total points          | 3,620,000|
|Survey area (m²)      |   4,064.9|
|Mean density (pts/m²) |     891.3|
|Mean height (m)       |      4.12|
|95th-pct height (m)   |     11.83|
|Voxel volume (m³)     |   1,483.2|


:::
:::


::: callout-note
**Representative result.** The values above are illustrative, generated to show
the shape of the output. Running the code on your own LAS file produces the same
table populated with your survey's metrics.
:::
